This description matches the conventional picture of a wave function Ψ( a′) that permeates the domain of all those attribute values a′ that are possible in theory, such as all spatial positions. 5 Summary and conclusions 5.1 Overview This paper offers a brief review of previous attempts to derive Born’s rule. It is pointed out that it is logically impossible to remove postulates like Born’s rule from quantum mechanics without replacing them with other postulates. However, the new postu- lates may have a different character than the old ones. A trend is identified where one or several formal postulates, like Born’s rule, are replaced with operational pos- tulates that describe the process of acquiring information by means of experiment, or by epistemic postulates that describe what the experimenter may know about the experiment. The basic idea of this paper is to follow this trend to its natural end, motivating Born’s rule by epistemic assumptions alone, without making any reference to any formal postulates of quantum mechanics. 26According to the crucial epistemic assumption, the structure of knowledge and the structure of the physical world reflect each other. This presumed circumstance may be called ontological closure. On the one hand, it means that a proper model of the physical world does not contain any elements or make any distinctions that are subjectively unknowable. On the other hand, it means that a proper physical model contains all fundamental forms of appearances and knowable distinctions. It is claimed that our potential knowledge is always incomplete. In a double-slit experiment, this means that knowledge about which slit the particle passes may forever be unknowable. In that case, a proper physical model should not have hypothetical precise paths as elements, according to the principle of ontological closure. As a con- sequence, probabilities should not be calculated as if there were such paths. Also, the model should distinguish between the two situations in which there is path knowl- edge, and in which there is not. This means that probabilities should be calculated differently in the two cases. In accordance with the above philosophy, probabilities are only defined in the present treatment in well-defined experimental contexts, where the outcome to which the probability is associated can actually be observed. Nevertheless, it is often possible to assign propensities to outcomes more broadly, by means of symmetry arguments or by application of physical law, regardless whether this outcome can actually be observed. In the double-slit experiment, for example, it is clear that the propensi- ties that the particle passes either slit are equal if the experimental arrangement is symmetrical, regardless whether it can ever be known which slit the particle actually passes. The crucial situation in which it is argued that Born’s rule is needed is when there are probabilities that are conditioned on propensities that are not probabilities themselves. The above considerations imply that the classical rules of probability cannot be applies in such cases, since they presuppose conditional probabilities referring to precise paths. To pinpoint Born’s rule among all conceivable alternative rules, it is assumed that there is a generally applicable physical law. In the present setting this means that there should be an algebraic scheme that can be used to calculate proba- bilities in all kinds of experimental contexts. The alternatives to Born’s rule provide too little parametric freedom to this end. 5.2 The main argument The crucial technical steps in the present motivation of Born’s rule are the following. The epistemic state of the experimental context C expressed in Eq. (17) after an attribute A has been attained, but before the subsequent attribute A′ is attained, is different from that in Eq. (19). In the former case one alternative Aj that increases the knowledge of the value of A is observed, whereas in the latter case it remains unknowable in principle which alternative was attained, and no knowledge about its value is gained. The assumption of ontological closure, discussed in section 3.1, implies that the probabilities for the alternatives {A′ j′} associated with A′ must be calculated differently in the two cases, as expressed by Eqs. (12) and (13). The assumption that the same general rule to calculate probabilities should nev- ertheless be applicable in both cases leads to a new normalization condition (39), 27in addition to those in Eq. (38), on the function f(x) that expresses this rule. The function f(x) is assumed to be such that its argument x is a representation of any propensity πx that is also a probability px. The choice f(x) = x is excluded since then the new normalization condition follows from the three classical ones, so that the two cases are treated in the same way, contrary to assumption. The extra normalization condition (39) also excludes real representations x of propensities, since the number of free parameters becomes too small to express the necessary degree of experimental freedom specified in section 3.2.5. The introduction of complex such representations cx doubles the number of free real parameters, making Born’s rule f(cx) = |cx|2 an acceptable choice. However, given the assumption expressed in section 3.2.6, any deviation from Born’s rule means that new conditions that relate the quantities {cjj ′} are introduced, like those in Eq. (44), again shrinking the set of independent parameters too much. It is not proved that an algebraic scheme involving Born’s rule can be applied in all relevant experimental contexts C. However, no counterexample has been found. Other acceptable algebraic schemes might be found if some assumptions are relaxed, such as Eqs. (15) and (29), or the assumption that propensities are represented by real or complex numbers. The existence of such an alternative scheme, which gives rise to different empirical predictions than that involving Born’s rule, would show that the present epistemic assumptions, together with the assumption that there is a generally applicable algebraic scheme, are insufficent to pinpoint correct physical law. 6 Outlook The epistemic approach to physics employed in this paper is a means to an end, namely to motivate Born’s rule. It is not promoted in and of itself. However, since it is argued that this philosophical ansatz is fruitful for this specific purpose, it may be worthwhile to try to use it to make sense of modern physics more broadly. Along this line, consider the symbolic representation P KC of the epistemic state of an experimental context C, which was introduced in section 4.1 in order to clarify the specific qualities of such contexts that are essential in the motivation of Born’s rule. It is possible to translate key parts of this formalism to states and operations in complex Hilbert spaces, akin to those used in standard quantum mechanics. To a complete set of M alternatives {Aj} it is possible to associate such a Hilbert space HA with dimension M, where each alternative Aj in this set corresponds to a unit vector |j⟩. The set {|j⟩} can be treated as an orthonormal basis for HA, so that ⟨j|k⟩ = δjk for the inner product ⟨·|·⟩ defined in HA. The association (14) between the complex quantity cj and Aj corresponds to the element cj|j⟩ in HA. The epistemic state [(c1|A1) ( c2|A2)] in Eq. (16) translates to a superposition |A⟩ = c1|1⟩ + c2|2⟩. Born’s rule gives ⟨A|A⟩ = 1, so that this state vector gets the appropriate unit length. The transition [(c1|A1) ( c2|A2)] → [Aj] from the epistemic state of attribute A in Eq. (16) to that in Eq. (17) when one alternative Aj is realized translates to a projection |A⟩ → ¯Pj|A⟩ = |j⟩ in HA. This is an expression of the measurement postulate of standard quantum mechanics. 28If two complete sets of alternatives {Aj} and {A′ j′} are defined in the same context C, it is natural to assign a Hilbert space HC = HA ⊗ HA′ with dimension M M′ to the context as a whole. This is so at least in the case where the corresponding attributes A and A′ are simultaneously knowable, that is, when their values are not related according to Heisenberg’s uncertainty principle. The final epistemic state [ Aj] [A′ j′] in Eq. (18) then corresponds to the tensor product |j⟩ ⊗ | j′⟩. The set of these product states form an orthonormal basis for HC. It is a long way to go from here to a construction of quantum mechanics as a whole, but the present perspective on Born’s rule unlocks a door for an attempt to achieve that goal. If this is indeed possible, the role of Hilbert spaces and wave functions would be to describe well-defined experimental contexts, rather than describing the state of the world at large. llm_extracted_info.main_contributions: Motivates (rather than strictly derives) Born’s rule from epistemic/operational assumptions without presupposing Hilbert-space postulates of quantum mechanics. Main problem: explain why probabilities in quantum experiments are computed as squared moduli of complex amplitudes and why classical probability composition fails when which-path information is unknowable. Key findings: (i) “ontological closure” (ontological completeness + minimalism) demands different probability calculus depending on knowability of alternatives (e.g., interference vs which-path). (ii) Requiring a generally applicable algebraic scheme for contexts with sequential alternatives plus independence/normalization constraints singles out complex representations and the map f(c)=|c|^2 as the only viable rule within the paper’s framework; higher-power rules (|c|^{2γ} with γ≠1) overconstrain parameters and fail to represent experimental freedom.
llm_extracted_info.methodology: Defines an epistemic framework where the physical state corresponds to collective potential knowledge PK(n). Formalizes experiments as contexts C containing complete sets of mutually exclusive/exhaustive alternatives with knowability levels (1–3). Introduces symbolic state expressions for a context as networks of alternatives with associated propensity-representatives c and a probability map p=f(c). Imposes algebraic modeling constraints: sequential composition modeled by multiplication; parallel/alternative composition by addition; “impossible” alternatives mapped to zero. Uses consistency across contexts with different knowability (including delayed-choice) to impose functional equation f(xy)=f(x)f(y) and multiple normalization conditions. Shows real-valued c insufficient; complex c yields family f(c)=|c|^{2γ}; then uses parameter-counting/independence constraints to rule out γ≠1, leaving Born’s rule.
llm_extracted_info.experimental_setup: No empirical experiments, datasets, or numerical benchmarks. Uses thought-experiment/gedanken analysis and standard interferometric scenarios (double-slit; Mach–Zehnder interferometer; delayed-choice and quantum eraser variants) represented as directed networks of alternatives. Validation is analytic: logical consistency with stated epistemic/operational assumptions, functional constraints on f, and parameter-counting to ensure sufficient degrees of freedom to represent independently tunable propensities/conditional propensities in a class of experimental contexts.
llm_extracted_info.limitations: Not a rigorous theorem-level derivation; depends on strong and sometimes informal philosophical postulates (ontological closure, identification of physical state with potential knowledge, discreteness of time parameter n). Assumes a specific modeling form p=f(c) and a ring-like algebra with addition/multiplication representing branching/sequential structure, plus the zero-implies-impossible rule. The “general applicability” claim is not fully proven; analysis mainly centers on two-stage contexts and extends via parameter counting; may require adding hypothetical unobserved alternatives (field-like extension). Condition M' ≥ M−1 indicates constraints for contexts where outcome cardinality decreases; relies on supplementing with unmeasured alternatives. Does not engage with empirical distinguishability from QM beyond reproducing Born’s rule; does not address dynamical/measurement-update postulates except suggestively.
llm_extracted_info.future_research_directions: (1) Formalize the argument as a rigorous theorem: precisely define the class of contexts, algebraic structures, and independence assumptions; prove uniqueness of Born’s rule beyond the analyzed cases. (2) Explore alternative schemes by relaxing assumptions (e.g., p not solely a function of single c; non-ring structures; representations beyond complex numbers) and determine whether any yield consistent, testable deviations. (3) Extend the epistemic-context formalism to reconstruct more of quantum theory (Hilbert spaces, tensor products, projective measurement update) and clarify when/why it emerges. (4) Analyze contexts with changing knowability (quantum erasers, adaptive measurements) more systematically and connect to contextuality/noncontextuality frameworks. (5) Investigate implications for continuous variables and the status of probability densities under ontological minimalism, possibly deriving continuum limits from finite-resolution alternatives.

research_hypothesis.open_problems: Prompt-only CoT improvements are rarely *calibrated*: a single fixed instruction (e.g., “think step by step”) or a fixed k for self-consistency forces the model to emit long rationales even when unnecessary, increasing cost and, more importantly, increasing *answer instability* (different samples yield different answers) and susceptibility to spurious intermediate steps. Existing prompt-only instance-adaptive methods typically gate CoT using shallow difficulty heuristics, but they do not (1) explicitly optimize for *decision stability* as a proxy for correctness under sampling, nor (2) provide a principled, prompt-only stopping rule that allocates extra reasoning only when uncertainty remains. This leaves a key gap: a fast, dataset-agnostic way to decide “when to stop thinking” that improves accuracy-per-token and robustness without any fine-tuning.
research_hypothesis.method:
Refine SCLoR into **A-SC2 (Adaptive Self-Consistency with Confidence- and Cost-Regularized Early Exit)**: a prompt-only algorithm that treats CoT sampling as a sequential decision process.

Core idea (human-like selective reasoning): start with minimal reasoning; only “think more” if the current answer distribution is unstable.

Algorithm:
1) Generate CoT samples sequentially (i = 1..K_max) using the same base prompt.
2) After each sample, extract final answer a_i and compute a *quality weight* w_i that downweights low-signal reasoning:
   w_i = exp(-λ·L_i) · (ε + c_i)
   where L_i is rationale length proxy, and c_i is a prompt-only self-contradiction flag (same cheap verifier as before).
3) Maintain weighted answer mass S(a)=∑ w_i for each answer.
4) Compute an uncertainty/stability measure from the current weighted distribution, e.g. normalized entropy H or margin m:
   p(a)=S(a)/∑_b S(b);
   margin m = p_top - p_second.
5) **Early-exit rule (novelty):** stop sampling when either
   (a) margin m ≥ τ (high confidence), or
   (b) H ≤ h0 (low entropy), or
   (c) i reaches K_max.
6) Output argmax_a S(a).

What’s novel vs standard self-consistency and the original SCLoR:
- Moves from *fixed-budget* aggregation to a **prompt-only compute allocation policy** with an explicit stability objective (margin/entropy).
- Jointly optimizes an accuracy–cost trade-off by combining (i) length-regularized, verifier-weighted votes and (ii) a sequential stopping criterion.
- Produces a measurable, socially relevant outcome: reduced token usage/latency while maintaining or improving accuracy, enabling more equitable access and greener inference.

Feasibility: all components (sampling, extraction, verifier prompt, and stopping) are implementable in a short Python script with any LLM API or local model.
research_hypothesis.experimental_setup:
Models: evaluate on (a) an API LLM and (b) one local open-weight model if available (e.g., a small instruct model), but keep conclusions prompt-only.

Datasets (quick but broader than GSM8K):
- GSM8K: 200 test questions (math word problems).
- SVAMP: 200 examples (math with adversarial rephrasing).
- StrategyQA (subset 200): multi-hop factual reasoning where overthinking can hurt.
(If time-constrained, run GSM8K+SVAMP only; keep code dataset-agnostic.)

Baselines:
1) Direct answer (no CoT).
2) Zero-shot CoT single sample.
3) Self-consistency majority vote with fixed k=10.
4) Original SCLoR fixed k=10 (length+verifier weighted vote, no early exit).

Proposed:
- A-SC2 with K_max=20, λ tuned on small dev (e.g., 50 GSM8K train/val), and τ tuned on dev over {0.10,0.15,0.20,0.25}.

Metrics:
Primary: accuracy.
Secondary:
- avg_tokens_generated (cost proxy).
- accuracy_per_1k_tokens.
- answer_instability: fraction of problems where first 5 samples contain ≥2 distinct extracted answers (lower is better).
- verifier_inconsistency_rate among sampled rationales.

Reporting:
- Compare accuracy at similar or lower token budgets.
- Additionally, plot accuracy vs average tokens by varying τ to show controllable trade-off.
research_hypothesis.expected_result:
Across 200-example subsets (per dataset), compared to fixed-k self-consistency (k=10):
- Accuracy: +1 to +4 points on GSM8K and SVAMP; neutral to +2 on StrategyQA (where long CoT can overfit/hallucinate).
  Example expectation (API LLM):
  - GSM8K: SC(10) 74–78% → A-SC2 77–81%.
  - SVAMP: SC(10) 68–74% → A-SC2 71–76%.
- Token cost: 25–45% fewer generated tokens on average vs SC(10) because many items stop early once margin is high.
- Accuracy_per_1k_tokens: +30–70% relative improvement.
- Answer_instability: decreases by ~10–25% (fewer problems with divergent sampled answers after weighting and early exit).

Higher is better for accuracy and accuracy_per_1k_tokens; lower is better for avg_tokens_generated and answer_instability.
research_hypothesis.expected_conclusion: A-SC2 supports a more human-like “stop when confident” reasoning policy under strict prompt-only constraints. By making self-consistency sequential and explicitly optimizing decision stability (margin/entropy) while regularizing verbose/inconsistent chains, it yields a better accuracy–cost frontier than fixed-budget CoT. Academically, it reframes prompt-only CoT as an adaptive inference/control problem with measurable stability signals, broadening evaluation beyond raw accuracy. Practically and socially, it reduces inference cost/latency and lowers the chance that spurious long rationales dominate decisions, enabling more efficient and reliable deployment without fine-tuning.
